findings.claim_under_attack: config/alerts.yml fires alerts on dropped messages, compaction backlog, and read/write p99 latency from the JMX-exported Cassandra metrics.
findings.finding: jmx-exporter.yml sets `lowercaseOutputName: true` (line 4), so every exported metric name is lowercased AFTER capture-group substitution. But 4 of the 8 alert expressions in alerts.yml reference CamelCase metric names that the exporter therefore never emits: `cassandra_dropped_Dropped` (l.29), `cassandra_compaction_PendingTasks` (l.39), and `cassandra_client_request_Latency_99thPercentile` (l.69, l.79). The real series are `cassandra_dropped_dropped`, `cassandra_compaction_pendingtasks`, `cassandra_client_request_latency_99thpercentile`. These four alerts (HCDDroppedMessages, HCDCompactionBacklog, HCDHighWriteLatency, HCDHighReadLatency) silently never fire. The smoking gun is in-repo: the Grafana dashboard queries the correctly-lowercased names (hcd-cluster.json:24,168,185), proving the author knows the exporter lowercases — only alerts.yml disagrees.
findings.strongest_rebuttal: Maybe Prometheus is case-insensitive, or another exporter emits the CamelCase series. False: PromQL metric-name matching is case-sensitive, jmx-exporter.yml is the only metric source (Dockerfile installs jmx_prometheus_javaagent; compose mounts only this file), and the dashboard's lowercased twins prove the emitted names are lowercase.
findings.what_would_resolve_it: Lowercase the metric names in alerts.yml to match the exporter output (e.g. cassandra_dropped_dropped, cassandra_compaction_pendingtasks, cassandra_client_request_latency_99thpercentile), exactly as the Grafana dashboard already does.
findings.lesson: Prometheus alert/dashboard exprs must reference metric names as the exporter actually emits them; with jmx_exporter `lowercaseOutputName: true` the final name (post capture-group substitution) is lowercased, so any CamelCase reference silently never matches. Cross-check every PromQL metric name against the jmx-exporter rules' lowercased output.

findings.claim_under_attack: tests/test_scripts.py validates the Prometheus alert rules.
findings.finding: test_prometheus_alerts_valid_yaml asserts only structural shape — that `groups` exists, there are >=5 rules, and each rule has `alert`/`expr`/`labels`/`severity`. It never checks that the metric name inside each `expr` matches what jmx-exporter.yml emits. So the test stays green while 4 of the 8 alerts (R2-01) are dead. This is a weak (non-discriminating) assertion: it cannot catch the very class of defect — a typo'd/mis-cased metric name — that most commonly breaks an alert rule.
findings.strongest_rebuttal: Without a live Prometheus you cannot fully validate alert firing. True, but the metric-name-vs-exporter consistency check is a pure offline string check (the jmx-exporter rules and the alert exprs are both static files), so the weak assertion is not forced by the offline constraint.
findings.what_would_resolve_it: Add an assertion that every metric name referenced in an alert `expr` resolves to a name producible by config/jmx-exporter.yml (or at minimum is all-lowercase, matching `lowercaseOutputName: true`).

findings.claim_under_attack: The hints alert and the hints dashboard panel monitor the same hint metric.
findings.finding: alerts.yml:59 uses `cassandra_hints_TotalHintsInProgress`, while the Grafana dashboard hints panel uses `cassandra_hints_hints_created` (hcd-cluster.json:219). These are two different names for the hint metric and neither is reconciled against the actual HintedHandOffManager MBean exposed by jmx-exporter.yml:46 (`cassandra_hints_$1`, lowercased). Beyond the casing problem of R2-01, the alert and dashboard disagree on which series even represents hints, so at least one (the alert) monitors a non-existent series.
findings.strongest_rebuttal: Both could coincidentally exist if HintedHandOffManager exposes both attributes. Unlikely and unverified — and `TotalHintsInProgress` is still CamelCase, so it is broken regardless by `lowercaseOutputName: true`.
findings.what_would_resolve_it: Pick the metric the exporter actually emits for hints and use the same lowercased name in both alerts.yml and the dashboard.

findings.claim_under_attack: .gitignore ignores the per-node local data directories (`hcd-node*`).
findings.finding: Line 40 of .gitignore is `[hcd-node*]`. The square brackets make this a POSIX character-class glob that matches a SINGLE character from the set {h,c,d,-,n,o,e,*}, not the intended `hcd-node1`/`hcd-node2`/... directory prefixes. `git check-ignore hcd-node1` returns NOT-ignored. The pattern is dead. (Impact is low because the compose stack uses Docker named volumes rather than host bind dirs, so these directories are not normally created — but the ignore rule does not do what it claims.)
findings.strongest_rebuttal: Named volumes mean no host `hcd-node*` dirs are created, so nothing leaks. True for the default topology, but a contributor using bind mounts or the topology generator's older layouts would have untracked node data silently un-ignored.

findings.claim_under_attack: The MinIO service is defined consistently across the demo and the compose stack.
findings.finding: The demo script starts MinIO at IP 172.28.0.40 with image tag RELEASE.2024-11-07T00-52-20Z (demo-entropy.sh:436,441), while docker-compose.yml assigns the `minio` service IP 172.28.0.10 with image RELEASE.2024-03-30T09-41-56Z (docker-compose.yml:183,188). Same logical service, two static IPs and two image tags. Not a hard failure (the demo runs its own `docker run`, and `make minio` runs the compose one — they are mutually exclusive paths), but it is an unsourced inconsistency: a reader using `make minio` then the ransomware modules gets a different MinIO than the modules' WORM proofs were written against.
findings.strongest_rebuttal: The two code paths never run simultaneously, so there is no IP collision and both images support Object Lock. Cosmetic.
findings.what_would_resolve_it: Single-source the MinIO IP and image tag (reuse the compose `minio` service, or share a variable), so demo and compose agree.
